top_risks.risk: No page-level runtime tests for LearnSession/Workspace/ReviewQueue pages
Route-state and request-key orchestration concentrated in one page
Context path split (`src/context` vs `src/contexts`) is stable but inconsistent
High-LOC core runtime files for session/event/workspace paths
Large AO/control script stack with shared dependency/counterparty risk
First-match route resolution without duplicate-path/method linting
Gateway validation mainly structural; payload-level validation is per-handler
Rate-limit and policy metadata incomplete for some high-impact routes
Legacy exclusion logic is explicit but static
MarkingResult / SmartMark boundary depends on explicit fallback posture
Mastery effect is distributed across event/attempt services, no single authoritative service file
Compatibility bridge behavior is active in workspace/review flows
Coverage collector excludes large runtime/AO/test surfaces outside API path
AO and advisory gates are hard to classify at a glance
No canonical workflow-intent mapping document was observed
Large scripts/test surfaces without dedicated governance index
Documentation gap for workflow semantics can slow reviewer triage
Runtime/tooling claims were noted by scan but not fully validated in this synthesis pass

top_25_findings.summary: Jest coverage is API-only while runtime and frontend tests sit outside coverage set
No page-level tests for three major learning-runtime pages
LearningSession page owns launch/import/ask orchestration plus route-state transitions
Context folder split (`src/context` and `src/contexts`) remains a maintenance risk
Route-to-page transition contracts lack explicit integration coverage
High-LOC backend runtime and service files indicate dense coupling
High import density in `api/rag/lib/ask-service.js`
High-LOC event pipeline service impacts scoring and attempt behavior
Core session-service contract and workspace services remain high-complexity risk points
AO controller scripts are large and operationally coupled
Large runtime test files add reviewer fatigue and refactor fragility
Large AO/state test fixtures can mask intent drift
Route-registry duplicates can silently shadow by first-match behavior
Gateway schema checks are not fully machine-level in `api/index.js`
Rate-limit metadata not comprehensive across all production routes
Legacy exclusions are static and require manual governance
No route registry matrix test for all non-learning module auth/rate combinations
Active scope bundle remains central and high-impact if altered
Anchor and registry path is primary for topic/question canonicality
Mastery effect is distributed, not a single module
Smart-marking boundary relies on explicit non-authoritative fallback
Legacy compatibility bridge behavior remains part of active flow
AO and advisory workflow semantics can be read as hard-gate failures
No canonical release/advisory/AO docs map for workflows
Large workflow/script testing surfaces without consolidated index increase governance effort

p0.title: Close coverage interpretation gap
acceptance_criteria: Document explicit policy for API-only coverage and its limits.
Define required runtime coverage evidence and reviewer expectation.
Add a follow-up validation path for frontend/runtime coverage drift.

p0.title: Add route-registry collision and policy-lint baseline
acceptance_criteria: Introduce deterministic duplicate path/method detection rule.
Validate existing registry set remains unchanged for auth/rate contracts.
Keep changes scoped to test/lint logic and documentation.

p2.title: Create scripts/docs index for test and workflow surfaces
Add complexity heatmap delta tracking to each future cleanup PR

open_questions: Whether coverage fix is code-gate-first or documentation-first
Whether route-lint is required hard-fail in this stage
Whether context path split is accepted or intentionally converged
Which 2-3 slices should be prioritized for first merge
assumptions: Input reports are complete and accurate for issues #461-#465
No direct repo files beyond provided reports were inspected during synthesis
Compatibility and typed-ref contract constraints remain mandatory during implementation
